Confidence is medium.

metrics.narrative: Value case is quantified and co-built. On 31 Mar 2026 Jordan Weimer framed $208M total spend, ~50% managed today targeting 90%, $14.5M unmanaged indirect spend for tail-spend automation, plus 3-5% savings and 1-7% from autonomous sourcing. By the 18 Jun final draft the conservative P2P model showed ~8 month payback and 5-7x five-year ROI, one point of managed spend ≈ $1M. The buyer engaged deeply on the math — the strongest signal here.

champion.narrative: Two engaged champions drive this: Jacquelyn Guerrero (Director, Packaging and Indirect Materials) and Rafal Prylinski (Manager, Indirect Spend) co-built the business case and will personally present to the EB. Engagement is active and recent (18 Jun). The gap is structural, not motivational: their advocacy is untested at the executive level and Zycus is not in the funding room — so champion enablement, not bypass, is the leverage.

competition.narrative: Competition is real but unnamed. Jacquelyn, 18 Jun: 'we've got multiple vendors that we're looking at... once we have funding it's about which vendor is right for Amy's.' The other alternative is status quo on Oracle. Zycus leads on the co-built case and warm champions but has zero visibility into competitor names, pricing, or timing. Win path: name the field via the champion, then lock the post-funding window with a staged proposal, peer references, and Oracle integration design.

identify_pain.narrative: Pain is specific and owner-attributed. Rafal, 31 Mar: indirect is largely uncontracted, MRO has 350+ vendors, the team wants to 'eliminate all these repetitive tasks.' Jacquelyn detailed manual MRO intake and AP review. The June restructure put ops/services and MRO under Rafal, which 'further cements the need for P2P.' AI to limit headcount is an executive priority.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting is downstream of funding and unowned. Jacquelyn, 18 Jun: 'we're still trying to figure out what we're doing with the contracting, to be honest.' No MSA/SOW, legal, or InfoSec sequence started. A mutual close/paper plan tied to the post-funding selection date would create visibility.

economic_buyer.narrative: EB is named but not met by Zycus. Jacquelyn, 18 Jun: 'Our chief supply chain officer, Oksana, will be her in July... we're gonna do a prerun with our VP of procurement.' Oksana controls funding. When Zycus offered to attend, Jacquelyn declined: 'she is most receptive to her internal team' — so the funding pitch is champion-carried by design. The path to power here is NOT a cold Zycus approach to Oksana; it is making the champions unbeatable in a room we cannot enter. A VP-to-EB offer, if made at all, should be routed through and offered by the champion, not pushed past the declined attendance.

decision_process.narrative: Clear, sequenced process per 18 Jun: pre-run with VP of Procurement, present to Oksana mid-July for funding, then run vendor selection, then an AP-team follow-up to finalize Oracle PO/invoice matching. Champions lead internally with the EB held in reserve.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are explicit: a conservative, defensible ROI executives can stand behind (Mike McCarthy, 31 Mar: 'a good, clean, defensible business case'); a 2-3 year ROI target the conservative model clears; Oracle coexistence via 3-way match; and AI/automation appetite to cut repetitive work and headcount. Jacquelyn praised the conservative model on 18 Jun. Scope A (P2P) vs Scope B (full source-to-pay) remains open.
